highlights: Twin Falls, a 287-foot waterfall connecting the upper and lower lakes
World-class fishing for rainbow, brook, and brown trout
Twin Lakes Path, an accessible trail offering scenic shoreline views
Charming footbridges providing unique vantage points and photo opportunities
Boat rentals for kayaks, canoes, and electric motorboats
Lakeside camping with 93-95 sites across three loops
attractions.tips: Arrive early as parking is limited and can fill up quickly. Be 'Bear Aware' and use provided bear boxes for food storage. Visit in May or June for the best waterfall flow during the spring melt. A California fishing license is required for angling
attractions.nearby: Mammoth Mountain Ski Resort, Lake Mary, Lake Mamie, Horseshoe Lake, Tamarack Lodge, John Muir Wilderness
attractions.seasonal_notes: Access is weather-dependent; campgrounds and stores typically open from late May (May 25) to early October (Oct 1); winter brings heavy snow and limited access

attractions.name: Wave Rave Snowboard Shop
attractions.description: Established in 1989, Wave Rave Snowboard Shop is a premier destination for snowboarding professionals and enthusiasts, offering a massive selection of gear, clothing, and expert advice. Owned by champion rider Steve Klassen, the shop provides top-tier rental packages and professional repair services. During the summer months, it transitions to a hub for electric bike and stand-up paddleboard rentals
attractions.address: 3203 Main St, Mammoth Lakes, CA 93546
attractions.hours: Sunday-Thursday: 7:30 AM - 8:00 PM; Friday-Saturday: 7:30 AM - 9:00 PM (Hours may extend during peak winter season)
attractions.admission: Free (Store Entry); Snowboard Rentals: Kids ($35), Basic ($55), Performance ($70), Demo ($85), Step On
highlights: Extensive inventory of snowboards, boots, bindings, and outerwear
Professional repair and tuning services including hot waxing and edge work
High-performance snowboard rental packages from brands like Burton and Jones
Summer rentals for electric bikes and stand-up paddleboards
Knowledgeable staff of dedicated riders and industry experts
Exclusive Wave Rave private label gear and collaborations
attractions.tips: Pick up rental gear after 3:00 PM the day before your ride to hit the slopes early. Check the shop for summer sales on winter apparel and equipment
attractions.nearby: Shea Schat's Bakery, Looney Bean Coffee, Mammoth Lakes Welcome Center, Mammoth Brewing Company, Mammoth Luxury Outlets
attractions.seasonal_notes: Open year-round; transitions from snowboarding focus in winter to biking and paddleboarding in summer

attractions.name: Wild Willy's Hot Springs (also known as Crowley Hot Springs)
attractions.description: Wild Willy’s Hot Springs is a natural geothermal site featuring multiple pools set against the dramatic backdrop of the Eastern Sierra mountains. The site includes a large heart-shaped pool and a tiered hot stream, offering temperatures ranging from 95°F to 105°F. It is one of the most popular and easily accessible hot springs in the Mammoth Lakes area, reachable via a short wooden boardwalk
attractions.address: Benton Crossing Road, Mammoth Lakes, CA 93546
attractions.hours: 24 Hours a Day
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Two main natural geothermal pools, including a unique heart-shaped one
Breathtaking 360-degree views of the Eastern Sierra Nevada and Glass Mountain Ridge
Elevated wooden boardwalk trail (0.25 miles) leading from the parking lot to the pools
Water temperatures ranging comfortably from 95°F to 105°F
Located on public BLM land allowing for a natural, undeveloped experience
Popular spot for stargazing due to low light pollution in the high desert
attractions.tips: Visit at sunrise or midweek to avoid large crowds. Practice 'Leave No Trace' principles by packing out all trash and avoiding the use of soaps or bath products in the natural pools. Note that clothing is optional and nudity is common. Bring plenty of water and a flashlight if visiting at night
attractions.nearby: Hot Creek Geological Site, Convict Lake, Whitmore Hot Springs, Crowley Lake
attractions.seasonal_notes: Open year-round; however, the access road may be closed or impassable during winter due to snow and mud, requiring a hike in. Best visited in late spring or early fall

attractions.name: Grant Lake
attractions.description: Grant Lake is the largest of the four lakes on the scenic June Lake Loop, spanning 1,100 acres and stretching over three miles. Known as the 'Home of the German Brown Trout,' it is a premier destination for trophy fishing, camping, and motorized watersports. Unlike other lakes in the loop, it permits high-speed boating and waterskiing after 10:00 am
attractions.address: Grant Lake Rd, Highway 158, June Lake, CA 93529
attractions.hours: Seasonal: Open from the last Saturday in April through mid-October. Marina/Store: Typically open dawn to dusk (boat rentals available 5am–Noon and 4pm–8pm); Quiet hours: 10pm–7am
attractions.admission: Free for general access; Boat Launch: $10; RV Camping: $36–$55/night; 14' Boat Rental: $75/day ($55 after 1pm); Paddle Board: $10/hr
highlights: Largest lake on the June Lake Loop at 1,100 acres
Renowned for trophy-sized German Brown Trout
Full-service marina with boat and paddleboard rentals
Only lake in the loop allowing waterskiing and wakeboarding
Marina campground with 70 sites for RVs and trailers
attractions.tips: A strict 10 mph speed limit is enforced on the lake before 10:00 am. The area has a high-desert feel with less shade than other loop lakes, and afternoon winds are common, making early morning the best time for fishing or calm water
attractions.nearby: Silver Lake, Gull Lake, June Lake, June Mountain Ski Area, Parker Lake Trail, and Fern Lake Trail
attractions.seasonal_notes: Summer only; the marina and campground operate from late April to mid-October

attractions.name: Gull Lake / Gull Lake Marina
attractions.description: Gull Lake is the smallest of the four lakes on the scenic June Lake Loop, offering an intimate alpine experience with dramatic backdrops like Carson Peak. Known as the most social lake on the loop, it features a popular marina, a well-stocked fishing environment, and family-friendly amenities such as a lakeside park and playground
attractions.address: 47 E Granite Ave, June Lake, CA 93529
attractions.hours: Marina: 7:00 AM – 6:00 PM during fishing season; Campground: Seasonal
attractions.admission: Free (Gull Lake Park and hiking); Boat rental prices vary (e.g., $25/hr for kayaks, $75+ for motorboats)
highlights: Smallest and most intimate lake on the June Lake Loop
Famous for "Monster Trout" fishing, including rainbow and brown trout
Gull Lake Marina offering pontoon, flat-bottom barge, kayak, and paddleboard rentals
Lakeside park with a children's playground, picnic areas, and tennis courts
Gull Lake Loop Trail, an easy 2-mile self-guided nature hike
Iconic rope swing on the west side for swimming
attractions.tips: Book boat rentals online in advance as they are popular; stop by the marina tackle shop for a free cup of coffee; use the self-guided nature trail pamphlet for the Gull Lake Loop hike; check the live webcam for real-time weather and lake conditions
attractions.nearby: June Lake, Silver Lake, June Mountain Ski Area, Reversed Peak Trail, June Lake Village
attractions.seasonal_notes: Seasonal; Marina and campground are open from late April (fishing opener) through October/early November. The area is also visited in winter for scenery and nearby skiing

attractions.name: June Lake Beach
attractions.description: June Lake Beach is an idyllic alpine destination featuring soft sand and crystal-clear swimming waters at an elevation of 7,500 feet. Located on the northern shore of June Lake, it offers breathtaking views of the surrounding Sierra Nevada peaks. It is a popular spot for sunbathing, picnicking, and non-motorized water activities like kayaking and paddleboarding
attractions.address: End of June Lake Beach Road, off Northshore Drive (GPS: 37.7961, -119.0696)
attractions.hours: Summer: 6:00 AM – 10:00 PM (Parking lot opens at 8:00 AM); Fall/Winter: Sunrise to Sunset. Gate closing times vary seasonally: 9:00 PM (late May–early Sept), 8:00 PM (early Sept), 7:00 PM (mid-Sept–mid-Oct)
attractions.admission: $10 per vehicle per day during spring and summer; $50 per vehicle per season; Free entry during fall and winter seasons
highlights: Sandy swimming beach with spectacular Sierra Nevada mountain views
Crystal clear alpine waters ideal for swimming and wading
Recreational opportunities including kayaking, stand-up paddleboarding, and canoeing
Picnic areas equipped with tables and vault toilets
World-class trout fishing for rainbow, brown, and brook trout
Handicap accessible parking and amenities in the first parking lot
attractions.tips: Parking is extremely limited with only 98 spaces; arrive early as the lot often fills up. Note that there is no potable water available at the beach, so bring your own. Dogs must be leashed in all developed recreation areas
attractions.nearby: June Mountain Ski Area, Gull Lake, Silver Lake, Grant Lake, and the town of June Lake
attractions.seasonal_notes: Open year-round; however, parking fees and extended hours apply during spring and summer. Some amenities and roads may be weather-dependent or closed in winter

attractions.name: June Lake Brewing
attractions.description: June Lake Brewing is a craft brewery and tasting room located in the scenic June Lake Loop of the Eastern Sierra. They have been brewing at altitude for over a decade, fostering a vibrant craft beer culture in the region. The brewery offers a variety of "super awesome beers" available in cans, growlers, and on tap
attractions.address: 131 S. Crawford Ave, June Lake, CA 93529
attractions.hours: Noon - 8:00 PM everyday
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Craft beer brewed at altitude
Awesome tasting room with a local vibe
Beer available in 16oz cans, 4 packs, and growlers
Part of the Eastern Sierra Ale Trail
On-site food truck options like La Parilla
Online webstore for beer and merchandise
attractions.tips: Pets are not allowed in the tasting room. Payment is accepted via credit card only; no cash. No private events or reservations are accepted
attractions.nearby: Tiger Bar, The Lift, T-Bar Social Club, Double Eagle Resort, June Lake Marina, Big Rock Resort, Silver Lake Resort
attractions.seasonal_notes: Open year-round; typically closes in November for annual maintenance

attractions.name: June Lake Loop
attractions.category: Natural site, Scenic loop, and Mountain village community
attractions.description: Affectionately known as the “Switzerland of California,” June Lake Loop is a spectacular 16-mile scenic drive along State Route 158 in the Eastern Sierra. The loop features four pristine alpine lakes—June, Gull, Silver, and Grant—nestled at the base of rugged peaks like Carson Peak. It offers a variety of year-round activities, from world-class trout fishing and hiking to family-friendly skiing at June Mountain
attractions.address: 2603 CA-158, June Lake, CA 93529
attractions.hours: Visitor Center: 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM daily. June Lake Beach: 6:00 AM – 10:00 PM (seasonal gate closures vary; parking lot opens at 8:00 AM). The North June Lake Loop road (SR 158 N) closes seasonally for winter
attractions.days_open: Visitor Center: Open Daily; Scenic Loop (South Junction): Year-round
attractions.admission: The scenic drive is Free. June Lake Beach (Day Use) requires a $10 fee per vehicle during spring and summer seasons, but is Free in fall and winter
highlights: Four pristine alpine lakes: June, Gull, Silver, and Grant
June Mountain Ski Area for family-friendly winter sports
World-class trout fishing and multiple full-service marinas
Stunning autumn foliage, particularly the brilliant golden Aspens
Quaint mountain village featuring the June Lake Brewing and local shops
attractions.tips: Arrive early at June Lake Beach as the parking lot fills quickly on summer weekends. Visit June Lake Brewing for local craft beer and check road conditions via Caltrans QuickMap during winter months
attractions.nearby: Mono Lake, Bodie State Historic Park, Yosemite National Park (via Tioga Pass), and Mammoth Lakes
attractions.seasonal_notes: The South Junction of the loop remains open year-round, while the North Junction typically closes in winter (Dec–Mar/Apr) due to snow. June Mountain Ski Area is open winter only, while many marinas and campgrounds are summer-specific

attractions.name: June Mountain Ski Area
attractions.description: June Mountain Ski Area is a premier winter resort in California’s Eastern Sierra, widely celebrated as "California's Family Mountain." It features 1,500 acres of uncrowded, beginner-friendly terrain and stunning views of the surrounding High Sierra peaks. The resort offers a relaxed, low-key atmosphere with 7 lifts and 41 trails, making it a favorite for families and locals alike
attractions.address: 3819 Hwy 158, June Lake, CA 93529
attractions.hours: Ticket Office: 7:30 AM – 4:30 PM; Chair J1: 8:00 AM – 4:00 PM; Other lifts: 8:30/9:00 AM – 4:00 PM
attractions.admission: Kids 12 and under ski/ride FREE. Adult day passes typically range from $85 to $129 depending on the date; youth and senior tickets range from $46 to $106. Scenic rides on Chair J1 are $25
highlights: Kids 12 and under ski and ride for free every day
1,500 acres of uncrowded slopes and family-friendly terrain
Spectacular panoramic views of the June Lake Loop and Sierra Nevada
Guided backcountry tours for intermediate and advanced skiers
Scenic Chair J1 ride to the June Meadows Chalet
attractions.tips: Arrive early to beat the morning rush on Chair J1, which is the only way to access the upper mountain. Purchase your lift tickets online in advance to save up to 30% off window prices
attractions.nearby: June Lake, Gull Lake, Mono Lake, Mammoth Mountain Ski Area, and the Tioga Pass entrance to Yosemite National Park
attractions.seasonal_notes: Open during the winter season (typically December through April). The resort is currently closed for the 25/26 season as of late April 2026

attractions.name: Silver Lake Resort
attractions.description: Established in 1916, Silver Lake Resort is the oldest fishing resort in the Eastern Sierra, situated on the banks of a picturesque alpine lake. The resort offers historic rustic cabins, a full-service RV park, a well-stocked general store, and a famous country cafe serving hearty mountain meals ()
attractions.address: 6957 Highway 158, June Lake, CA 93529
attractions.hours: Store: 7 am – 7 pm Daily; Cafe: 7 am – 2 pm Daily. Note: The resort is seasonal; for 2026, it opens April 24
attractions.admission: Free for general area access; RV sites: $62/night; Boat rentals: $50–$85; Kayak rentals: $25/hour
highlights: Oldest fishing resort in the Eastern Sierra (est. 1916)
Full-service marina with boat and kayak rentals
Historic rustic cabins and 80-site RV park
On-site General Store and Silver Lake Cafe
Prime trout fishing access at Silver Lake and Rush Creek
Live webcams providing real-time views of the lake and dock
attractions.tips: Reservations for cabins and RV spots are handled exclusively via phone and should be booked months in advance for summer. Visitors should remain 'bear aware' by properly storing all food and coolers. Bring warm clothing as temperatures at 7,200 feet can drop significantly at night
attractions.nearby: June Lake, Gull Lake, Grant Lake, Rush Creek, and Inyo National Forest hiking trails
attractions.seasonal_notes: Summer only; typically open from late April through mid-October

attractions.name: Lee Vining, California
attractions.description: Lee Vining is a historic community located at the base of the Sierra Nevada mountains on the southwest shore of Mono Lake. It serves as the primary eastern gateway to Yosemite National Park via Tioga Pass and is a hub for outdoor recreation including fishing, hiking, and exploring unique geological features. The town is famous for its proximity to ancient saline waters and the dramatic tufa towers of the Mono Basin
attractions.address: Hwy 395 and 3rd St, Lee Vining, CA 93541
attractions.hours: Town accessible 24/7; Mono Basin Scenic Area Visitor Center typically open 9:00 AM – 5:00 PM daily (seasonal closures apply in winter)
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: Proximity to Mono Lake and its ancient tufa towers
Eastern gateway to Yosemite National Park via Tioga Pass
Access to Bodie State Historic Park, a well-preserved ghost town
Home to the famous Whoa Nellie Deli at the Tioga Gas Mart
Premier destination for ice climbing in Lee Vining Canyon
Spectacular fall foliage viewing in nearby Lundy and Lee Vining Canyons
attractions.tips: Stop by the Mono Basin Scenic Area Visitor Center for interactive exhibits and a film about the lake's ecology. Don't miss a meal at the Whoa Nellie Deli, known for its gourmet food in a gas station setting. Check the Tioga Pass road status before travel if visiting in spring or late fall
attractions.nearby: Mono Lake, Bodie State Historic Park, Yosemite National Park, June Lake Loop, Lundy Lake
attractions.seasonal_notes: Open year-round; however, Highway 120 (Tioga Pass) into Yosemite is closed in winter, typically from November through May or June

attractions.name: Lundy Canyon / Lundy Canyon Trail
attractions.description: Lundy Canyon is a spectacular high-elevation canyon in the Eastern Sierra known for its vibrant quaking aspens, cascading waterfalls, and rich mining history. The area features a variety of natural wonders including beaver ponds, lush riparian ecosystems, and dramatic granite peaks that transition into the Hoover Wilderness
attractions.address: Lundy Canyon Road, Lee Vining, CA 93541 (Trailhead: 38.0224, -119.262)
attractions.hours: 24 hours (Trail access); Seasonal road closures apply in winter
attractions.admission: Free (Day use); Wilderness permit required for overnight trips
highlights: Stunning fall colors with extensive quaking aspen groves
Multiple cascading waterfalls including the Lundy Canyon Falls
Active beaver ponds and diverse wildlife like Sierra Nevada bighorn sheep
Historical mining relics and old cabins from the region's gold mining era
Access to the high-altitude 20 Lakes Basin and Hoover Wilderness
attractions.tips: Visit in October for the best fall colors or late spring/early summer for peak waterfall flow. Be prepared for a dirt road to the trailhead and bring plenty of water as there is no potable source at the canyon
attractions.nearby: Mono Lake, Lee Vining, Bodie State Historic Park, Yosemite National Park (Tioga Pass), June Lake Loop
attractions.seasonal_notes: Road access is typically available from mid-May through mid-October; the area is subject to heavy snow and winter closures

attractions.name: Mono Lake Tufa State Natural Reserve
attractions.description: Mono Lake is an ancient saline lake, over 1 million years old, famous for its otherworldly tufa towers—calcium-carbonate spires formed by the interaction of freshwater springs and alkaline lake water. It serves as a critical habitat for millions of migratory birds and is one of the oldest lakes in North America
attractions.address: Highway 395, Lee Vining, CA 93541
attractions.hours: Day Use: Sunrise to Sunset; Visitor Center: Summer 8:00 AM – 5:00 PM daily, Spring/Fall 8:30 AM – 4:30 PM Thu-Mon, Winter Closed
attractions.admission: $3.00 per person for ages 16 and older; Free for children 15 and under; Federal Interagency Passes (e.g., America the Beautiful) are accepted
highlights: Tufa Towers: Spectacular calcium carbonate formations along the shore
Ancient Saline Ecosystem: One of the oldest lakes in North America, 2.5 times saltier than the ocean
Birdwatching: A vital resting and feeding stop for millions of migratory birds
South Tufa Area: Features the highest concentration of towers and a 1-mile interpretive loop trail
Photography: Renowned for its unique light, mountain reflections, and dramatic scenery
Boating: Human-powered vessels like kayaks and canoes are permitted for exploring the lake
attractions.tips: Wear layered clothing as the weather is highly changeable, and carry plenty of water and sunscreen. Do not climb or stand on the fragile tufa towers, and visit the South Tufa area for the best up-close views
attractions.nearby: Bodie State Historic Park, Yosemite National Park (Tioga Pass), June Lake Loop, Panum Crater, Lundy Canyon
attractions.seasonal_notes: Open year-round, though State Route 120 East (access to South Tufa) may be closed in winter due to snow. The Visitor Center is closed during the winter season

attractions.name: Tioga Pass Entrance
attractions.description: Tioga Pass is a high mountain pass in the Sierra Nevada and the highest highway pass in California at an elevation of 9,945 feet. It serves as the spectacular eastern gateway to Yosemite National Park, connecting the Eastern Sierra to the park's high country. The route offers breathtaking views of granite peaks, alpine meadows, and pristine subalpine lakes
attractions.address: Tioga Road (Highway 120), Yosemite National Park, CA; GPS: 37.9111, -119.2581
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours daily during the season
attractions.admission: Standard Pass: $35 per vehicle (valid for 7 days); Non-U.S. Residents (16+): $100 per person surcharge (new for 2026); Annual Pass: $80 (U.S. residents) / $250 (Non-residents)
highlights: Tuolumne Meadows - one of the largest high-elevation meadows in the Sierra Nevada
Tenaya Lake - a stunning alpine lake popular for picnicking and photography
Olmsted Point - offers iconic views of Half Dome and Clouds Rest from the east
Mount Dana - the second highest peak in Yosemite, rising directly east of the pass
Lembert Dome - a prominent granite dome providing panoramic views of the high country
Dana Meadows - scenic meadows located right at the pass summit
attractions.tips: Arrive early in the morning or late in the afternoon to avoid heavy traffic and ensure parking. Note that the entrance station is cashless and accepts major credit cards or mobile payments only. Services are extremely limited along Tioga Road, so bring plenty of food and water
attractions.nearby: Mono Lake Tufa State Natural Reserve, Bodie State Historic Park, June Lake Loop, and Lee Vining
attractions.seasonal_notes: Summer Only; Tioga Road typically opens in late May or June and closes in November due to snow

attractions.name: Virginia Lakes
attractions.description: Virginia Lakes is a high-altitude basin in the Eastern Sierra featuring nearly a dozen pristine alpine lakes set at elevations above 9,000 feet. The area is a premier destination for trout fishing and hiking, offering three drive-to lakes and a network of trails leading into the Hoover Wilderness. It is also home to a historic resort and a popular campground, making it an accessible yet tranquil mountain getaway
attractions.address: Virginia Lakes Road, Bridgeport, CA 93517
attractions.hours: Trailhead: Open 24 hours; Resort and Campground: Typically open Memorial Day weekend through mid-October, weather permitting
attractions.admission: Free for day use and hiking; fees apply for camping and resort lodging
highlights: Nearly a dozen alpine lakes within the basin
Three drive-to lakes: Big Virginia, Little Virginia, and Trumbull
Exceptional trout fishing for rainbow, brook, and brown trout
Gateway to the Hoover Wilderness and Yosemite National Park trails
Historic Virginia Lakes Resort featuring cabins and a general store
Vibrant fall foliage and dramatic mountain scenery
attractions.tips: Prepare for the high altitude (9,700+ feet) by acclimating and staying hydrated. Gas-powered boats are prohibited to maintain the area's tranquility. Bear-resistant food storage is required for campers and hikers
attractions.nearby: Mono Lake, Bodie State Historic Park, Tioga Pass (Yosemite), Travertine Hot Springs, Bridgeport
attractions.seasonal_notes: Summer only; the access road and facilities are closed in winter due to heavy snow and high elevation

attractions.name: Ancient Bristlecone Pine Forest
attractions.description: The Ancient Bristlecone Pine Forest is a high-altitude protected area in the White Mountains that preserves some of the world's oldest living non-clonal organisms. These Great Basin bristlecone pines, some exceeding 5,000 years of age, thrive in harsh conditions on alkaline dolomite soil. The forest features gnarled, twisted trees that have served as critical records for climate and carbon dating research
attractions.address: White Mountain Road (via Highway 168), Bishop, CA 93514
attractions.hours: Visitor Center: 10:00 AM – 4:00 PM (Summer season); Forest Site Access: 6:00 AM – 10:00 PM (Daily during season)
attractions.admission: $3.00 per person, maximum $6.00 per car; children under 18 are Free; Interagency Passes (America the Beautiful) are accepted
highlights: Schulman Grove: Home to the Methuselah Tree, one of the oldest living trees on Earth
Patriarch Grove: Located at 11,000 feet, featuring the Patriarch Tree, the world's largest bristlecone pine
Methuselah Trail: A 4.5-mile loop through the oldest groves with panoramic views of the Sierras
Discovery Trail: An accessible 1-mile loop with interpretive signs explaining the forest's history
Visitor Center: Modern facility at Schulman Grove offering exhibits, a documentary film, and ranger-led programs
Stargazing: Renowned for exceptionally dark skies due to its high elevation and remote location
attractions.tips: No water or gas is available on-site; fill up in Big Pine and bring plenty of drinking water. Be prepared for high altitudes (10,000+ ft) by walking slowly and staying hydrated. Temperatures can be 30 degrees cooler than in the valley, so dress in layers
attractions.nearby: Grandview Campground, Big Pine, Manzanar National Historic Site, Owens Valley, Laws Railroad Museum
attractions.seasonal_notes: Seasonal only; closed for winter from October-April due to snow. Typically open from mid-May through November, weather permitting

attractions.name: Bishop Creek Canyon
attractions.description: Bishop Creek Canyon is a magnificent 30-square-mile wilderness area in the Sierra Nevada, featuring the North, Middle, and South Forks of Bishop Creek. Each fork terminates at a stunning alpine lake—North Lake, Lake Sabrina, and South Lake—surrounded by granite peaks and dense forests. It is a premier destination for high-altitude recreation, including world-class trout fishing, hiking into the John Muir Wilderness, and spectacular fall color viewing
attractions.address: Highway 168 W (West Line Street), Bishop, CA 93514
attractions.hours: Canyon access is open 24/7; most campgrounds and resorts operate seasonally from late April through October/November
attractions.days_open: Daily (seasonal road access to higher elevations)
attractions.admission: Free for general access; Campground fees range from $24 - $34 per night
highlights: Three major alpine lakes: North Lake, South Lake, and Lake Sabrina
Exceptional trout fishing for Rainbow, Brown, and Brook trout
World-renowned fall foliage displays from extensive quaking aspen groves
Access to the John Muir Wilderness and major trailheads like Bishop Pass
12 USFS campgrounds and several historic mountain resorts
Dramatic views of 13,000+ foot peaks along the Sierra Crest
attractions.tips: Visit in late September or early October for peak fall colors. Use the provided bear boxes for all food storage as the area is active bear habitat. Be prepared for steep, narrow roads, especially the final approach to North Lake. Wilderness permits are required for all overnight trips into the adjacent backcountry
attractions.nearby: Town of Bishop, Laws Railroad Museum, Buttermilk Country bouldering, Keough’s Hot Springs, Rock Creek Canyon
attractions.seasonal_notes: The canyon is most active in summer and fall; roads to high-altitude lakes and most facilities close for the winter season (typically late October to April) due to heavy snow

attractions.name: Bishop Mule Days Celebration
attractions.description: Bishop Mule Days is a six-day world-class equestrian event and festival promoting the legacy and heritage of the mule. It features 14 mule shows, world championships in various disciplines, and what is billed as the "World's Longest Non-Motorized Parade" through Main Street
attractions.address: Tri-County Fairgrounds, Sierra Street, Bishop, CA 93514 (Office: 1141 N. Main St, Bishop, CA 93514)
attractions.hours: Events typically start at 7:00 AM and continue into the evening; Fairgrounds office is open 9:00 AM – 4:00 PM
attractions.days_open: Tuesday through Sunday (6 days) preceding Memorial Day
attractions.admission: Fairgrounds entrance is free; Arena shows range from $20 (General Admission) to $140 (Box/Grandstand Seats); Dance tickets are $25
highlights: The World's Longest Non-Motorized Parade along Bishop's Main Street
14 Mule Shows featuring over 500 mules in disciplines like dressage and packing
Country Music Concerts and community dances
Arts and crafts fair and local vendor marketplace
"I Want To Be A Packer" booth and activities for children
World-class specialty acts and comedy classes
attractions.tips: Purchase tickets early as arena shows often sell out; wear comfortable boots and a wide-brimmed hat for sun protection; tour the barns for free to see the mules up close
attractions.nearby: Erick Schat’s Bakkery, Bishop City Park, Laws Railroad Museum, Keough’s Hot Springs
attractions.seasonal_notes: Summer Only

attractions.name: Eastern California Museum
attractions.description: Founded in 1928, the Eastern California Museum preserves and interprets the cultural and natural history of Inyo County and the Eastern Sierra. The museum features extensive collections of Native American artifacts, regional photography, and exhibits on pioneer life and the Los Angeles Aqueduct. It is operated by the County of Inyo and supported by the Friends of the Eastern California Museum
attractions.address: 155 N. Grant St., Independence, CA 93526
attractions.hours: Tuesday–Sunday: 10:00 AM – 5:00 PM; Closed Mondays and major holidays
attractions.days_open: Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday, Sunday
attractions.admission: Free; a $5 donation is appreciated
highlights: One of the state's most extensive collections of Owens Valley Paiute and Timbisha Shoshone basketry
Restored Southern Pacific #18 "Slim Princess" narrow-gauge steam locomotive
Extensive exhibits and photographs from the Manzanar War Relocation Center
The Mary DeDecker Native Plant Garden showcasing local flora
Exhibits detailing the "Water Wars" and the history of the Los Angeles Aqueduct
An archive of over 27,000 historic photographs of the Eastern Sierra region
attractions.tips: Visitors should allow at least 1-2 hours to view the extensive indoor galleries and outdoor equipment displays. There is a museum bookstore on-site and a picnic area for visitors to enjoy. For researchers or large groups, it is recommended to contact the museum in advance to schedule your visit
attractions.nearby: Manzanar National Historic Site, Mount Whitney Trailhead, Laws Railroad Museum, Ancient Bristlecone Pine Forest
attractions.seasonal_notes: Open year-round

attractions.name: Laws Railroad Museum and Historical Site
attractions.description: Laws Railroad Museum and Historical Site is an eleven-acre indoor and outdoor museum preserving the history of the Owens Valley and its railroad heritage. Located at the site of the original 1883 railroad depot, it features a collection of nearly 50 historic buildings and numerous artifacts from the pioneer era. The centerpiece of the museum is the "Slim Princess" Southern Pacific Narrow Gauge Locomotive No. 9 and its associated rail cars (,)
attractions.address: 200 Silver Canyon Road, Bishop, CA 93514-9505
attractions.hours: Summer (June–August): 9:30 am – 4:00 pm daily; Winter (September–May): 10:00 am – 4:00 pm daily. Closed Thanksgiving, Christmas, and New Year's Day
attractions.admission: $10 per person suggested donation; children under 12 are free. Train rides (on select dates) are $8 for adults, $4 for children under 18, and free for children 3 and under
highlights: Southern Pacific Narrow Gauge Locomotive No. 9 ("Slim Princess")
Original 1883 Laws Railroad Depot and restored Station Agent’s House
Nearly 50 authentic historic structures filled with pioneer artifacts
Original 20-Mule Team Borax wagons and mining equipment displays
Narrated train rides on select dates using a 1927 Brill car
Working ore processing mill and skip car demonstrations
attractions.tips: Allow at least 2–3 hours to explore the extensive 11-acre site. Leashed pets are welcome on the grounds and even on the train rides. It is recommended to pack a lunch as there are limited food options on-site despite ample parking and picnic space (,)
attractions.nearby: Bishop City Park, Volcanic Tablelands, Ancient Bristlecone Pine Forest, Keough's Hot Springs, and the Owens Valley Paiute Shoshone Cultural Center
attractions.seasonal_notes: Open year-round; hours vary slightly by season. Train rides are available only on specific dates throughout the year

attractions.name: Mount Whitney
attractions.description: Mount Whitney is the highest peak in the contiguous United States, standing at an elevation of 14,505 feet. Located on the boundary of Inyo National Forest and Sequoia National Park, it features the world-famous Mount Whitney Trail, a strenuous 22-mile round-trip trek that rewards fit hikers with breathtaking alpine panoramas. The peak's dramatic eastern face and unique jagged ridges make it one of the most iconic destinations in the Sierra Nevada
attractions.address: Whitney Portal Road, Lone Pine, CA 93545 (trailhead) or 640 South Main Street, Lone Pine, CA 93545
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours daily, though access is limited by permit and seasonal road closures
attractions.admission: Quota season (May 1 – Nov 1) requires a $15 per person recreation fee plus a $6 reservation fee; non-quota season (Nov 2 – Apr 30) requires a free permit with a $6 reservation fee
highlights: Highest point in the contiguous United States
Southern terminus of the 211-mile John Muir Trail
Strenuous 22-mile round-trip Mount Whitney Trail for hikers
Historic Smithsonian Institution Shelter (stone hut) at the summit
Stunning views of the Owens Valley and Sequoia National Park
attractions.tips: Acclimate to high altitude in Lone Pine or at Whitney Portal before starting your hike; start before sunrise to avoid afternoon thunderstorms; carry and use required bear-resistant containers and human waste pack-out kits
attractions.nearby: Alabama Hills, Whitney Portal Store and Waterfall, Lone Pine Lake, Manzanar National Historic Site, Eastern Sierra Interagency Visitor Center
attractions.seasonal_notes: Permit quotas are in effect from May 1 to November 1; the trail is typically snow-free from July to late September, while winter travel requires mountaineering skills

attractions.name: Owens Valley
attractions.description: Owens Valley is a deep, arid graben valley located between the Sierra Nevada to the west and the White and Inyo Mountains to the east. It is world-renowned for its dramatic landscapes, including volcanic tablelands and alpine meadows, and its historical significance as a primary water source for Los Angeles via the Owens River-Los Angeles Aqueduct. The region serves as a premier destination for outdoor enthusiasts, offering world-class fishing, climbing, and hiking (,)
attractions.address: 37.3735403, -118.3773316 (GPS coordinates); Visitor information at 609 N. Main Street, Bishop, CA 93514
attractions.hours: Accessible 24 hours daily; Owens Valley Station is open year-round
attractions.admission: Free
highlights: One of the deepest valleys in the United States, flanked by 14,000-foot peaks
The Owens River, a world-class destination for trout, catfish, and bass fishing
Rich history of the 'California Water Wars' and the Los Angeles Aqueduct
Gateway to the High Sierra, including proximity to Mount Whitney, the highest peak in the contiguous US
Unique geological features like the Volcanic Tablelands and ancient bristlecone pines nearby
attractions.tips: Renting an RV is a popular way to explore the valley's wide-open spaces and camp under the stars. Be sure to visit the Museum of Western Film History in Lone Pine to see where countless classic Westerns were filmed (,)
attractions.nearby: Mount Whitney, Ancient Bristlecone Pine Forest, Manzanar National Historic Site, Mono Lake, and Mammoth Lakes
attractions.seasonal_notes: Open year-round; summer is ideal for high-altitude hiking and fishing, while winter is the prime season for bouldering and desert exploration

attractions.name: Owens Valley Paiute-Shoshone Cultural Center
attractions.description: The Owens Valley Paiute-Shoshone Cultural Center is a museum and living center dedicated to preserving the history, culture, and lifeways of the Nüümü (Paiute) and Newe (Shoshone) people. It features curated collections of artifacts, historical archives, and beautifully illustrated exhibits showcasing traditional food sources, clothing, and the renowned art of basket weaving. The facility also includes a Memorial Hall honoring Native American veterans and serves as a community gathering place to continue traditional ways
attractions.address: 2300 West Line Street, Bishop, CA 93514
attractions.hours: Summer (April - September): Tue-Fri 10am-6pm, Sat-Sun 10am-3pm; Winter (October - March): Tue-Sat 10am-4pm
attractions.admission: Free (Donations are highly appreciated and gratefully accepted)
highlights: Curated collections of traditional artifacts, tools, and clothing
Extensive exhibits on the traditional art of Paiute basket weaving
Memorial Hall dedicated to Native American Veterans and their families
Native Garden and Walking Trail through the Conservation Open Space Area
Gift shop featuring authentic arts and crafts made by local tribal artisans
Interactive science area and educational displays for children
attractions.tips: Photography is strictly prohibited inside the building to respect the sacred nature of the artifacts. Be sure to explore the outdoor Native Garden and walking trail, and check the gift shop for unique local tribal crafts. Donations are the primary way the center sustains its operations, so contributing is encouraged
attractions.nearby: Laws Railroad Museum and Historic Site, Bishop City Park, Keough's Hot Springs, Ancient Bristlecone Pine Forest
attractions.seasonal_notes: Open year-round with seasonal hours of operation; outdoor gardens and trails are particularly scenic in the fall

attractions.name: Twin Lakes Resort - Bridgeport, California
attractions.description: Twin Lakes Resort is a historic vacation destination set on 36 acres within the Toiyabe National Forest at the base of the Sierra Nevada mountains. The resort is renowned for its trophy trout fishing on Lower Twin Lake and offers a variety of accommodations including cabins and RV camping. Guests can enjoy a full-service marina, a general store, and the 'Off the Hook' Deli & Bar in a scenic alpine setting
attractions.address: 10316 Twin Lakes Road, Bridgeport, California 93517, United States
attractions.hours: Store: 6:00 AM - 6:00 PM; Deli & Bar: 8:00 AM - 6:00 PM; Boat Docks: 6:00 AM - 4:00 PM
attractions.admission: Free to visit; prices vary for boat rentals, cabin stays, and RV sites
highlights: Trophy trout fishing, home to state record Brown Trout
Full-service marina with aluminum boat, pontoon, kayak, and SUP rentals
On-site 'Off the Hook' Deli & Bar serving breakfast, lunch, and specialty cocktails
General store and tackle shop fully stocked for fishing and camping needs
Direct access to scenic hiking trails including the Barney Lake trail
Annual 'Fish Fest' fundraiser featuring a fishing tournament and live concert
attractions.tips: Reservations for cabins and RV sites should be made up to a year in advance as they fill up quickly. There is no cell service or Wi-Fi in the campgrounds, but free Wi-Fi is available for guests at the general store. Be sure to check the resort's live webcam for real-time weather and lake conditions before your trip
attractions.nearby: Travertine Hot Springs, Buckeye Hot Springs, Bodie State Historic Park, Mono Lake, Robinson Creek, and the town of Bridgeport
attractions.seasonal_notes: Open seasonally from mid-April through October 31st; closed during the winter months

attractions.name: Bodie State Historic Park
attractions.description: Bodie State Historic Park is a genuine California gold-mining ghost town preserved in a state of "arrested decay." Visitors can walk down deserted streets that once housed a thriving community of 10,000 people, with interiors left exactly as they were when residents departed. It is a National Historic Landmark located in the high desert of the Sierra Nevada mountains (,)
attractions.address: Highway 270, Bridgeport, CA 93517
attractions.hours: Summer (Memorial Day - Labor Day): 9:00 AM - 6:00 PM; Winter (Labor Day - Memorial Day): 9:00 AM - 4:00 PM
attractions.admission: Adults (18+): $8.00; Children (4-17): $5.00; Infants (0-3): Free; $3.00 for Self-Guided Tour Book
highlights: Authentic ghost town preserved in "arrested decay"
Over 100 historic buildings including a church, schoolhouse, and barbershop
Standard Stamp Mill (guided tours usually available in season)
Remote, high-elevation setting at 8,375 feet in the Bodie Hills
Historic cemetery and mining artifacts scattered throughout the site
attractions.tips: Bring cash for the self-pay station and tour books. There are no commercial facilities (no food, fuel, or water for sale) in the park. Wear comfortable walking shoes and bring sun protection as the site is fully exposed at high elevation. The final 3 miles of the road into the park are unpaved and can be rough (,)
attractions.nearby: Mono Lake, Bridgeport, June Lake Loop, Yosemite National Park (East Entrance), Travertine Hot Springs
attractions.seasonal_notes: Open year-round; however, vehicle access via SR 270 is typically only possible in summer and shoulder seasons. In winter, the park is accessible only by skis, snowshoes, or snowmobiles due to heavy snow ()

attractions.name: Death Valley National Park
attractions.description: Death Valley National Park is a land of extremes, featuring below-sea-level basins, towering snow-capped peaks, and rare fields of wildflowers. As the hottest, driest, and lowest national park in the U.S., it offers a diverse environment of salt flats, sand dunes, and deep canyons. It is the largest national park in the contiguous United States, protecting a vast wilderness area across the California-Nevada border
attractions.address: P.O. Box 579, Death Valley, CA 92328 (Physical Address), Furnace Creek Visitor Center: CA-190, Death Valley, CA 92328
attractions.hours: Park: Open 24 hours daily; Furnace Creek Visitor Center: 8:00 AM – 5:00 PM daily
attractions.admission: Private Vehicle: $30.00; Motorcycle: $25.00; Per Person: $15.00; Annual Pass: $55.00
highlights: Badwater Basin: The lowest point in North America at 282 feet below sea level
Mesquite Flat Sand Dunes: Expansive and dramatic dunes easily accessible for exploration
Artist's Palette: Volcanic hills known for their striking, multi-colored pastel hues
Zabriskie Point: Famous panoramic overlook featuring iconic erosional landscapes
Dante's View: A premier overlook providing sweeping views of the entire valley
Ubehebe Crater: A massive volcanic crater on the northern end of the park
attractions.tips: Plan for extreme heat by carrying plenty of water and visiting popular sites at sunrise or sunset. The park is cashless, so ensure you have a credit or debit card for fees. Cell service is very limited; download maps and information before arriving
attractions.nearby: Rhyolite Ghost Town, Ash Meadows National Wildlife Refuge, Alabama Hills, Lone Pine, and Tecopa Hot Springs
attractions.seasonal_notes: Open year-round, but extremely hot in summer; winter and spring are the most popular visiting seasons. Some higher elevation roads may close in winter due to snow
